text:
PARIS ГўВҖВ” ItГўВҖВҷs Sunday night at Stade de France, and Yaroslava Mahuchikh is here but not here. On the other end of the stadium, 12 men are trying to medal in the hammer throw; on the track, runners in three events are trying to advance to the finals; in MahuchikhГўВҖВҷs hometown, Ukrainian troops are trying to save their country.

ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs not possible to focus only on track, to high jump,ГўВҖВқ she will say later, when she is asked if she can clear her mind when she competes. ГўВҖВңOur families in Ukraine ГўВҖВҰ we have friends who are fighting ГўВҖВҰ. I cannot forget about this. I remember, always.ГўВҖВқ

The high jump bar is set at 1.86 meters to start. In early July, Mahuchikhbroke the 37-year-old world record, jumping 2.10 meters. The next day, Russia bombed five Ukrainian cities, including Dnipro, where Mahuchikh grew up. Mahuchikh knows she can jump higher than 1.86 meters, so she passes on this round. The bombs killed at least 31 people. Mahuchikh makes her first jump, at 1.91 meters, and clears the bar on her first attempt. One of the bombs hit a childrenГўВҖВҷs hospital.

The bar is raised to 1.95 meters. ГўВҖВңA lot of rocket attacks to my city,ГўВҖВқ Mahuchikh will say later. Three jumpers fail to clear 1.95; eight remain. ГўВҖВңWe had a lot of alarms and a lot of explosions in my region,ГўВҖВқ she says.

The bar is raised to 1.98 meters. ГўВҖВңWe fight to represent our country, and to show people we are strong,ГўВҖВқ Mahuchikh will say. Six jumpers fail to clear 1.98; only Mahuchikh and AustraliaГўВҖВҷs Nicola Olyslagers remain. Between jumps, Olyslagers opens her journal and writes her thoughts. Between jumps, Mahuchikhclimbs in a sleeping bagto calm her mind..

The bar is raised to 2.0 meters. When Russia invaded, Maruchikh packed belongings in her car and fled Ukraine, and now she is not there but she is there. Maruchikh clears 2.0 meters on her first attempt; Olyslagers knocks the bar down on her first two. Four days ago, there were reports of explosions in Dnipro.

Clap ГўВҖВҰ clap ГўВҖВҰ

Olyslagers puts her hands together to get the crowd going. For the fans at Stade de France who showed up primarily for the menГўВҖВҷs 100-meter final, Olyslagers is a revelation. Before each jump, she looks like a giggly kid who doesnГўВҖВҷt think she can blow out all the candles on her birthday cake. She talks to herself and doesnГўВҖВҷt care how it looks; everybody in the stadium can see her lips move on the big screens. She grins after each jump, whether she was successful or not, and she has a smile as wide as the sea.

Clap ГўВҖВҰ clap ГўВҖВҰ

On her third attempt, Olyslagers clears 2.0. This doesnГўВҖВҷt happen often, but it has already happened here: Two Ukrainains and two Australians have medaled in this event. UkraineГўВҖВҷs Iryna Gerashchenko and AustraliaГўВҖВҷs Eleanor Patterson share bronze. AustraliaГўВҖВҷs Olyslagers and UkraineГўВҖВҷs Mahuchikh will get gold and silver, in some order.ГӮ

The bar is raised to 2.02 meters. Olyslagers fails to clear it on her first two attempts. But so does Mahuchikh. Olyslagers needs to succeed on her third attempt to have a chance at gold; otherwise, Mahuchikh will win because she has needed fewer attempts to get here.

ГўВҖВңI want to say that of course we won medals for our Ukrainian people and our defenders,ГўВҖВқ Mahuchikh will say later. Olyslagers lifts off. ГўВҖВңWe want peace,ГўВҖВқ Mahuchikh will say. Olyslagers knocks the bar over.

Yaroslava Mahuchikh has won Olympic gold. On the other side of Stade de France, Mykhaylo Kokhan, another native of Dnipro, has taken bronze in the hammer throw. Mahuchikh, Kokhan and Gerashchenko carry the Ukraine flag around the stadium to celebrate.

Joy takes many forms, and we need them all. At the medalistsГўВҖВҷ press conference after the high jump, two Australians and two Ukrainians smile and compliment each other and try to make the most of their time behind microphones. Olyslagers encourages kids to be patient as they chase their dreams. Manuchikh urges people to remember her country.ГӮ

The silver medalist says, ГўВҖВңGive yourself at least 10 years.ГўВҖВқ The gold medalist says, ГўВҖВңSometimes I forget that IГўВҖВҷm only 22.ГўВҖВқ

Michael Rosenberg is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated, covering any and all sports. He writes columns, profiles and investigative stories and has covered almost every major sporting event. He joined SI in 2012 after working at the Detroit Free Press for 13 years, eight of them as a columnist. Rosenberg is the author of "War As They Knew It: Woody Hayes, Bo Schembechler and America in a Time of Unrest." Several of his stories also have been published in collections of the year's best sportswriting. He is married with three children.

Follow rosenberg_mike

title: Yaroslava Mahuchikh Wins Ukraine's First Individual Gold at Paris Olympics
description: After fleeing her country due to the war with Russia, the 22-year-old high jumper dedicated her victory to her people.
